text:
Foreign policy opinions in America have long been viewed as capricious and irrational by scholars. In recent years, however, opinions on foreign policy have been viewed by scholars as being based on stable attitudes. Scholars have identified a range of factors including demographic, ideological and partisan factors as affecting foreign policy attitudes. However, religion has rarely been examined despite being frequently mentioned by politicians and commentators in relation to foreign policy attitudes. This paper will examine attitudes of different religious groups, based on data from the 2012 Chicago Council on Global Affairs public opinion survey, and position each group on Wittkopf’s militant internationalism (MI) and cooperative internationalism (CI) indices.

Americans who believe in militant internationalism — who believe that the United States in the world must rely heavily on force and action and that Washington should have the right to act unilaterally to promote its own interests — tend to fall into one of two religious groups. The first includes Evangelical Protestants, along with more conservative Jews, Catholics and Mormons. The second includes various minority ethnoreligious groups, as well as what can be called "modernist" or "progressive" religions. At the same time, the base for those who believe in cooperative internationalism — who rely more on diplomacy, multilateral institutions, and foreign aid to achieve U.S. objectives abroad — tends to be quite different. These include certain minority ethnoreligious groups, as well as modernist/progressive religious adherents and, especially, secular people. These findings suggest that historical ethnoreligious factors as well as culture war factors have a profound effect on the way Americans — and American public opinion — view the role of their country in the wider world.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
